summary: This is an advice column by 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil) responding to reader questions about graduate school applications, transitioning from academia to industry while maintaining teaching opportunities, and statistical analysis of gender bias in hiring. The column mixes playful humor with substantive advice on navigating academic careers and understanding research methodology. The most substantive discussion concerns a study claiming male PIs hire fewer female postdocs, where O'Neil critiques the statistical methodology and explains why observational studies cannot establish causation without randomized experiments. She notes that while she's skeptical of this particular study's proof, randomized experiments have consistently shown systemic sexism exists in hiring.
key_points: When applying to PhD programs, reaching out to professors is normal and flattering - they like taking students and appreciate genuine interest in their work
Leaving academia doesn't mean abandoning teaching - options include tutoring, summer programs, adjunct positions, and data science training programs
Observational studies about hiring bias suffer from confounding factors that make causal claims difficult to support
Small sample sizes in social science research cannot produce the high-sigma significance levels that physicists expect from their experiments
Despite methodological critiques of individual studies, randomized experiments have consistently demonstrated systemic sexism in hiring
money_quotes: Physicists are kind of spoiled for data. They often just collect way more data than other people can, and their experiments don't typically affect the results nearly as much, nor are they as messy, as you see in human experiments.
This paper seems to think there's a natural experiment going on, but there almost never is. There are almost always confounding factors.

If we really wanted to say men are less willing to hire women, we'd need to set up a randomized experiment and send a bunch of resumes that differ only in the gender of the applicant, and see what happens next.
Every time a randomized experiment has been performed, to my knowledge, there has been systemic sexism in place.
You can never get a 5-sigma results when your n is small.
At the end of the day math is a community of people, and the sooner you start getting to know the people the better.
key_insight: Observational studies with small samples and confounding factors cannot prove causation, but the consistent results from properly randomized experiments suggest systemic bias is real even when individual studies are methodologically flawed.
